content:
Most people use AI to polish their resume. A few use it to rebuild it from scratch. Here’s the difference.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There is a gap between a resume that looks good and a resume that works.
A lot of job seekers are closing that gap with AI, but most of them are using it wrong. They paste their resume into ChatGPT or Claude and ask it to “make this sound better.” They get back a polished version of the same mediocre document. Still weak foundations, just better grammar.
The people actually landing interviews are doing something different. They are using AI as a thinking partner, not a copy editor. They are prompting it to stress-test their positioning, rewrite bullets with real impact, and tailor every version of their resume to the specific job they actually want.
This article gives you the prompts to do that properly.

Before You Start: One Thing to Understand
Claude is only as useful as the information you give it. For resume work, that means two things: paste your actual resume content, and include the job description you are targeting. The more context you provide, the better the output.
With that said, here are the 15 prompts.
The Core Prompts
1. Audit your current resume
Here is my current resume: [paste resume]. I am applying for this role: [paste job description]. Audit my resume honestly. Tell me the three biggest weaknesses, what a hiring manager would think in the first 10 seconds, and what is missing that the job description is clearly looking for.
This is where you start, not with polishing. You need to know what is actually broken before you fix anything.

2. Rewrite weak bullet points
Here are my job experience bullet points for [role/company]: [paste bullets]. Rewrite each one using the STAR format (Situation, Task, Action, Result). Where I have not included metrics, flag it and suggest what kind of numbers I should try to add.
Vague bullets are the number one reason resumes get ignored. This prompt forces specificity.

3. Tailor your resume to a specific job
Here is my resume: [paste resume]. Here is the job description: [paste JD]. Rewrite my resume summary and top three bullet points from my most recent role to directly mirror the language and priorities in this job description. Keep my experience accurate but adjust emphasis to match what they are clearly looking for.
Recruiters are scanning for keywords and relevance. This prompt does the alignment work.

4. Write a resume summary from scratch
Here is my background: [brief description of your experience, years, industry, key skills]. Write three versions of a resume summary: one focused on my technical skills, one focused on my results and impact, and one that bridges both. Each should be 2 to 3 sentences max.
Most people write their summary last and treat it as an afterthought. It is the first thing a recruiter reads.

5. Identify keyword gaps
Here is the job description: [paste JD]. Here is my resume: [paste resume]. List every keyword, skill, or phrase in the job description that does not appear in my resume. Then tell me which of those I can legitimately add based on my actual experience, and where to place them.
ATS systems filter resumes before a human ever reads them. This prompt helps you pass the first gate.

6. Translate your experience into business language
I work in [field/industry]. Here are my responsibilities in technical terms: [paste description]. Translate this into business language that a non-technical hiring manager or recruiter would understand and find impressive. Focus on value and outcomes, not jargon.
Especially useful for engineers, data professionals, and anyone applying outside their immediate industry.

7. Quantify your impact
I need help adding metrics to these bullet points: [paste bullets]. I do not have exact numbers for everything. For each bullet, suggest what type of metric would make it stronger (time saved, revenue impact, team size, percentage improvement) and help me estimate a reasonable range based on the context I give you.
You do not need perfect numbers. You need directionally accurate ones that make your impact concrete.

8. Rewrite your skills section
Here is my current skills section: [paste]. Here is the job description I am targeting: [paste JD]. Reorganize and rewrite my skills section so the most relevant items appear first. Remove anything that adds no value for this specific role. Suggest any missing skills I should consider adding if I genuinely have them.
Most skills sections are unordered lists dumped onto the page. This prompt makes them strategic.

9. Create a career change resume
I am transitioning from [current field] to [target field]. Here is my background: [paste resume or summary]. Help me reframe my experience for a [target role] position. What transferable skills should I emphasize? How should I handle the fact that I have no direct experience in the new field? Write a revised summary and suggest how to reorder or retitle my experience sections.
Career transitions require a fundamentally different framing approach, not just swapping out keywords.

10. Write a project section
I want to add a projects section to my resume. Here are projects I have worked on: [describe 2 to 3 projects]. For each one, write a concise project entry with a title, a one-sentence description, key technologies or methods used, and the outcome or impact. Format it cleanly for a resume.
Particularly useful for students, recent graduates, or anyone whose job titles do not tell the full story.

11. Prepare for gaps in your resume
I have a [X month/year] gap in my employment history between [date] and [date]. During this time I was [brief description of what you were doing]. How should I address this on my resume? Should I include an explanation or leave it out? Write a short entry or note I could include if appropriate.
Gaps are less of a problem than how you handle them. This prompt helps you work through the framing before a recruiter forces you to.

12. Write an entry-level resume with minimal experience
I am applying for my first [type of role] job. I have: [list education, internships, projects, relevant coursework, volunteer work, certifications]. Build a strong one-page resume structure for me. Prioritize what matters most for someone without full work experience in this field, and write compelling bullets for each section I have content for.
No experience does not mean no value. It means you need a smarter structure.

13. Compare two versions of your resume
Here are two versions of my resume summary / bullet point / skills section. Version A: [paste]. Version B: [paste]. Which is stronger for a [target role] position and why? What would you change about the weaker version?
When you are stuck between two options, let Claude play editor.

14. Simulate a recruiter review
You are a senior recruiter at a [type of company] reviewing candidates for a [target role] position. Here is the job description: [paste JD]. Here is my resume: [paste resume]. Give me your honest assessment as that recruiter. Would you advance me to the next round? What would make you hesitate? What stands out positively?
This is the stress test. It forces an outside perspective on what you have built.

15. Generate a master resume
I am creating a master resume that I will tailor for different applications. Here is everything about my background: [paste full work history, education, skills, projects, achievements]. Organize this into a comprehensive master resume with all sections clearly labeled. Do not cut anything yet. I want the full version I can pull from when applying to different roles.
A master resume is not what you send. It is your source document. Every tailored application pulls from this.

How to Use These Together
You do not need all 15 prompts for every application. A realistic workflow looks like this: start with the audit prompt to know what you are working with, run the keyword gap analysis for each role you apply to, use the tailoring prompt to adjust your summary and top bullets, and finish with the recruiter simulation to catch anything you missed.
That is maybe 30 to 45 minutes of focused work per application. Not bad for what used to take days.

One Last Thing
Claude is genuinely good at resume work, but it cannot invent your experience. The prompts above only work if you are feeding them real, honest information about what you have actually done. The goal is not to create a fictional version of yourself. It is to make sure the real version comes across clearly.
That is harder than it sounds, and that is where these prompts help.
